feedback.text: In spirit of what is trying to be accomplished with the 'Digital Fairness Act' I would like to suggest to add; Preservation of purchased digital and physical products (video games). In this era of subscriptions and live services, video games are mostly unregulated. This has led to many products being sold to the consumer (video games in particular) that have been effectively terminated sometime after sale without any reimbursement or recourse on this matter. Essentially stealing and destroying products that consumers paid for and have often also bought products in through means of microtransactions. This issue is of such a grave matter that there is a EU Citizens initiative called 'Stop Killing Games' with over 1.430.000 signatures as of me writing this plea. Corporations have been destroying our purchased products (video games) for nearly a decade now by ending their playability through enforcing their EULA we were forced to agree to. There is, as of now, no recourse to stop them ending a live service game that we have bought as a product and that we have bought products in through microtransactions. There needs to be a point to start holding these corporations to some accountability. There is no excuse to let them continue in this unregulated way any longer than they already have, they have been exploiting consumers and skirting by regular consumer laws for far too long. Please consider legislating a way in which the products (video games) we have bought, digitally or physically; Should be left in a playable state when terminating (live) service.
